text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Post-Hospitalization Toxicity Assessment — Immune-Related Colitis, Returning Patient

ATTENDING ONCOLOGIST: [Attending Physician]
DEPARTMENT: Medical Oncology / Melanoma Program

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Ms. Okonkwo is a 55-year-old woman with metastatic melanoma (Stage IV, M1c, BRAF V600E+) presenting for follow-up approximately 3 weeks following discharge from a 7-day hospitalization for Grade 3 immune-related colitis. She is here to assess toxicity resolution, review steroid taper progress, and plan resumption of anti-cancer therapy.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Okonkwo has a known history of metastatic melanoma, initially diagnosed with widespread disease involving skin, bilateral axillary and inguinal lymph nodes, and multiple hepatic metastases. She was initially treated with dabrafenib (Tafinlar) 150 mg BID plus trametinib (Mekinist) 2 mg daily, achieving a marked partial response but developing acquired resistance at approximately 8 months with new cervical nodal, hepatic, and peritoneal disease. Liquid biopsy at the time of progression demonstrated emergence of a MAP2K1 C121S resistance mutation and BRAF amplification. Given her high TMB (28 mut/Mb) and the rationale for combination immunotherapy, she was transitioned to ipilimumab (Yervoy) 3 mg/kg plus nivolumab (Opdivo) 1 mg/kg IV q3 weeks per the CheckMate-067 design.

She completed cycles 1 and 2 without major issue — she did develop a Grade 1-2 maculopapular rash over the trunk and upper extremities after cycle 2, managed with topical triamcinolone and diphenhydramine, which resolved without interrupting therapy. Three days after receiving cycle 3 (approximately 9 weeks into ipi/nivo), she presented to the ED with 8-10 watery stools per day, severe crampy lower abdominal pain, and low-grade fever to 38.1°C. C. diff and stool cultures were negative. Flex sig revealed diffuse mucosal erythema, edema, and ulceration from the rectum through the sigmoid; biopsies confirmed immune-mediated colitis with lymphocytic and neutrophilic infiltrate and crypt abscesses. Both ipi and nivo were held. She was admitted and started on IV methylprednisolone 1 mg/kg/day. After partial response over 3 days (stools improving to 4-5/day), she received infliximab 5 mg/kg IV with rapid clinical improvement — stools down to 2-3/day above baseline within 48 hours. She was transitioned to oral prednisone 1 mg/kg/day and discharged after 7 days with a structured 8-week steroid taper plan.

Today she presents 3 weeks post-discharge. She reports stool frequency has returned to her baseline of 1-2 formed stools per day. No more cramping, urgency, hematochezia, or mucus per rectum. No nausea or vomiting. She is tolerating a regular diet without difficulty. Energy levels are gradually recovering though she notes still some mild fatigue compared to her pre-hospitalization baseline. She has not had any fevers since discharge. No rash recurrence. No joint pain, dyspnea, or vision changes.

She is currently on prednisone 10 mg/day (from the taper, down from 1 mg/kg); she has been tapering by approximately 10 mg every 1-2 weeks as instructed. Weight is stable — she notes she may have lost about 2-3 lbs during the hospitalization but has regained this.

She asks about when immunotherapy can be restarted. She is anxious about the cancer given the prior progression on targeted therapy and has concerns about leaving the disease untreated for an extended period.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
- Constitutional: Mild fatigue, gradually improving. No fevers, chills, or night sweats since discharge.
- GI: Stool frequency normalized to 1-2/day, formed, no blood or mucus. No abdominal pain. No nausea. Appetite intact and improving. Denies dysphagia.
- Respiratory: No cough, no dyspnea, no pleuritic chest pain. (Monitoring for pneumonitis given prior immunotherapy.)
- Dermatologic: Prior truncal/proximal extremity rash fully resolved. No new rash lesions. No oral ulcers.
- Musculoskeletal: No new joint swelling or arthralgia.
- Neurologic: No headaches, focal weakness, or sensory changes.
- Endocrine: Reports mild fatigue — thyroid labs pending review (pre-existing hypothyroidism, on levothyroxine). No palpitations, heat/cold intolerance beyond baseline.
- Ophthalmologic: No new visual symptoms (history of remote anterior uveitis; stable per recent ophthalmology note).
- Genitourinary: No hematuria, no dysuria. Urine output normal.
- Hematologic: No unusual bruising or bleeding.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vitals: BP 118/74 mmHg, HR 72 bpm (regular), RR 16, Temp 37.0°C, SpO2 98% on room air, Weight 68.4 kg (stable from pre-hospitalization).
Performance Status: ECOG 1.

General: Alert, well-appearing woman in no acute distress. Affect is anxious but engaged.
Skin: No active maculopapular rash. Surgical scars on left forearm and right shin from prior SCC excisions — healing well without signs of recurrence. No new cutaneous or subcutaneous nodules palpated over the trunk or extremities. No skin breakdown. Mild Cushingoid features consistent with ongoing corticosteroid therapy (mild facial rounding, no striae).
HEENT: No oral ulcers. No scleral icterus. Pupils equal and reactive. No cervical lymphadenopathy — the previously noted right level II cervical node is no longer palpable (a favorable sign). No thyromegaly.
Lymph nodes: Right axillary: residual small, non-tender node, substantially reduced from prior — not clinically impressive. No left axillary, inguinal, or supraclavicular adenopathy palpated.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs. No lower extremity edema.
Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No crackles, wheeze, or rhonchi.
Abdomen: Soft, non-distended. No tenderness on palpation, including the right upper quadrant. No hepatomegaly appreciated on examination, though exam is limited by body habitus. No palpable peritoneal masses. Bowel sounds normoactive.
Musculoskeletal: No joint effusions. Full range of motion in upper and lower extremities.
Neurologic: Grossly intact. No focal deficits.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (obtained today):
CBC: WBC 7.8 K/uL (ANC 5.2), Hgb 12.1 g/dL (mild normocytic anemia, stable), Plt 241 K/uL.
Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
  - AST 38 U/L (previously 68 U/L at time of ipi/nivo initiation — notable improvement)
  - ALT 41 U/L (previously 72 U/L — improving)
  - Total bilirubin 0.6 mg/dL (normal)
  - Alkaline phosphatase 82 U/L (normal)
  - Albumin 3.8 g/dL
  - Creatinine 0.82 mg/dL (normal; monitoring for nephritis)
  - Sodium 139 mEq/L, Potassium 3.9 mEq/L (normal on corticosteroid taper)
LDH: 241 U/L (down from 389 U/L at progression on dabrafenib/trametinib — a favorable trend that likely reflects early immunotherapy response even on just 3 cycles).
TSH: 3.1 mIU/L (within acceptable range on levothyroxine 75 mcg; no dose adjustment needed at this time).
Urinalysis: Negative for protein, blood, casts. No evidence of immune nephritis.
C-reactive protein: 8 mg/L (mildly elevated, non-specific in context of resolving colitis and steroid taper; trending down from discharge value of 22 mg/L).

Note: Autoimmune hepatitis workup (anti-smooth muscle Ab, ANA, anti-LKM) sent at the time of hospitalization returned negative, making immune-mediated hepatitis unlikely. The improvement in LFTs is reassuring and likely reflects a combination of resolution of prior hepatic metastatic burden with early immunotherapy response, rather than hepatitis.

---

IMAGING:
No new imaging obtained today. Most recent imaging was from the staging CT performed approximately 14 weeks after ipi/nivo start (obtained just prior to this visit cycle, results reviewed previously). At that time, the scan showed mixed but overall favorable response — reduction in right cervical node (2.2 → 1.4 cm), right axillary node (2.8 → 1.6 cm), hepatic lesions (largest 1.4 → 1.0 cm), with stable peritoneal nodule. No new disease. LDH trending down. First restaging imaging post-colitis will be scheduled as formal restaging once she is closer to resuming therapy.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

1. METASTATIC MELANOMA (Stage IV M1c, BRAF V600E+, PTEN loss, high TMB, MSS):
   - Disease status: Deferred — formal restaging imaging not yet repeated post-hospitalization. Indirect markers (LDH 241 U/L, falling; LFTs improving) are encouraging and suggest early immunotherapy activity from the 3 cycles completed.
   - Patient has received 3 of planned 4 induction cycles of ipilimumab/nivolumab. Given Grade 3 immune colitis, IPILIMUMAB IS PERMANENTLY DISCONTINUED. This is consistent with standard of care for Grade ≥3 colitis. The 4th cycle of ipi will not be administered.
   - Plan to resume NIVOLUMAB (Opdivo) monotherapy 480 mg IV every 4 weeks as maintenance, pending:
     a) Prednisone taper to ≤10 mg/day (she is at 10 mg today — very close)
     b) Full clinical resolution of colitis symptoms (stool at baseline for ≥2 weeks — she meets this criterion)
     c) Confirmation of no active hepatitis or other active irAE
   - Given that she is at exactly 10 mg prednisone today and the colitis appears fully resolved clinically, I will plan to begin the nivo taper to 5 mg prednisone over the next 2 weeks and then restart nivolumab at her next scheduled visit (approximately 2 weeks from today), contingent on continued colitis remission and taper success.
   - Prior to resuming nivo, will recheck LFTs, CBC, CMP, and urinalysis.
   - First restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis to be performed approximately 8-10 weeks after resuming nivolumab.

2. GRADE 3 IMMUNE-RELATED COLITIS (resolved):
   - Currently on prednisone 10 mg/day. Will taper by 5 mg/day over 2 weeks (to 5 mg/day for 1 week, then discontinue if asymptomatic).
   - Reinforce with patient: return to ED immediately for any recurrence of ≥4 stools/day above baseline, blood in stool, significant abdominal pain, or fever.
   - Gastroenterology follow-up confirmed and scheduled. GI team aware of plan to restart nivolumab and will co-manage.
   - Infliximab given once during hospitalization; patient should avoid live vaccines and be monitored for infection over the coming months.
   - Given the colitis history, if diarrhea recurs with nivolumab rechallenge, will have a low threshold for prompt endoscopy vs. empiric steroid initiation. Patient education reinforced today: when in doubt, call the on-call line before the situation escalates.

3. HYPOTHYROIDISM (pre-existing, on levothyroxine 75 mcg):
   - TSH 3.1 mIU/L — currently well-controlled. No dose change needed.
   - Will recheck thyroid function at the nivolumab restart visit and then every 6-8 weeks while on nivo given the risk of immune-related thyroid dysfunction (worsening hypothyroidism or thyroiditis).
   - Endocrinology aware.

4. PRIOR CUTANEOUS SQUAMOUS CELL CARCINOMAS (x2, both excised, on BRAF inhibitor therapy — now off that therapy):
   - Dabrafenib discontinued at 8 months; the paradoxical SCC risk associated with BRAF inhibition is no longer operative now that she is off vemurafenib/dabrafenib class drugs.
   - Nonetheless, dermatologic surveillance maintained every 3 months for at least 1 year to ensure no recurrence of SCC at excision sites and to assess for any new melanoma primaries or suspicious nevi.
   - Most recent derm surveillance: clear.

5. PERFORMANCE STATUS / SUPPORTIVE CARE:
   - ECOG 1 today — mild fatigue attributed to steroid taper, post-hospitalization recovery, and underlying disease. Should improve as steroids are weaned and if immunotherapy continues to work.
   - Continue acetaminophen PRN for discomfort. Avoid NSAIDs given colitis history.
   - Dietary recommendations: continue low-residue diet cautiously, transitioning back to regular diet as tolerated. Nutritional support offered — patient declined, states appetite good.
   - Blood glucose monitoring: patient aware that prednisone can cause hyperglycemia; she has been checking glucose at home. Will monitor fasting glucose at next visit.
   - Social work consulted during hospitalization; patient reports good social support at home. Partner attends appointments.

6. DISCUSSION WITH PATIENT:
   - Extensive time spent today reviewing the rationale for permanent ipi discontinuation versus restarting nivo. Patient initially expressed concern about receiving "less" treatment, but understands the toxicity risk of further ipilimumab exposure. The data from CheckMate-067 supporting nivolumab monotherapy maintenance were reviewed.
   - The encouraging LDH trend and improving LFTs were highlighted as evidence that even 3 cycles of combination therapy may have generated a meaningful immune response.
   - Patient verbalizes understanding and agreement with the plan. She was counseled to report any new symptoms immediately.
   - She asked specifically about return to work — discussed that pending continued colitis remission and recovery from steroids, gradual return to her landscape architecture work should be feasible and is encouraged.

Return to clinic: 2 weeks for pre-nivo bloodwork and assessment; nivolumab restart planned if criteria met. Next full restaging imaging in approximately 10 weeks from restart of nivo.

Time spent: 45 minutes (>50% in counseling and coordination).

[Electronically signed — Attending Oncologist, Melanoma Program]
